result:
The hum begins in the walls. Not an electrical hum, though it’s easy to mistake it for one. It’s the hum of a life being lived in the apartment next door, a life you’ve never seen but have learned in intimate, frustrating detail. The scrape of a chair at 7:03 PM. The three distinct knocks of what you assume is a knee against a low coffee table, nightly. The faint, tinny melody of a radio talk show host you can never quite make out. You have built them, this neighbor, from these sounds. You’ve decided they are a man, retired, meticulous, with a fondness for boiled eggs at 6:30 AM (the specific rattle of the saucepan) and solitaire after dinner (the soft, rhythmic slide of cards).

You are a curator of their unseen existence. Your own life feels, in comparison, silent. Your movements don’t hum; they whisper. Your thoughts are moths batting softly against the glass of your skull.

***

Today, you find a button in the elevator. A simple, black, four-hole shirt button, resting in the dust and lint of the corner. You pick it up. It is cool and smooth. It is undeniably *from* something. A coat? A favorite cardigan? It feels like a clue. You pocket it. The hum next door that evening has a new quality—a slight agitation. A drawer is opened and shut with more force than usual. You clutch the button in your palm and wonder: is he looking for this?

This is how mythology starts. With a button.

***

You begin to leave things in the elevator. Not as restitution, but as communication. A single green apple, polished to a high shine. A postcard of a mountain you’ve never visited, with nothing written on the back. A spool of red thread. You are building a narrative between the two of you, a story told in objects and inferred meanings. You imagine him finding them, turning them over in his hands—hands you’ve decided are broad, weathered, with careful nails. You imagine his confusion turning to curiosity, then to a quiet understanding. A game. A shared, silent game.

The hum changes. The chair is scraped less. The solitaire sessions grow shorter. You hear a new sound: the soft creak of floorboards by his front door, as if he’s listening for the elevator’s groan, for the moment the offering is placed.

***

One Tuesday, there is something for you. A small, carved wooden bird, its wings smoothed by time. It sits exactly in the center of the elevator floor. Your heart does something strange, a bird-like flutter against your ribs. You take it. That night, you place your palm against the wall you share, as if feeling for a pulse. You swear you feel an answering warmth.

The exchange accelerates. You receive a pressed autumn leaf in a paperback book (page 72). You leave a single chess piece, a white knight. He returns a matchbox with a tiny, perfect feather inside. You are no longer curating his life; you are co-authoring a phantom friendship. Your own apartment begins to feel less silent. It is filled with the potential of the next object, the next chapter. You talk aloud to your wall sometimes. “Did you like the sea glass?” “Where did you find this fossil?” The hum, now, is a language.

***

It is inevitable that the game must escalate. The objects must become more personal, more meaningful. The elevator must become a gallery of your two souls. You decide to leave the ultimate offering: a key. Not a key to anything, just a small, old-fashioned skeleton key from a junk shop. But its symbolism is immense. It says: *I trust you with an entrance. I give you a means to unlock something.*

You place it on the elevator’s cold floor and retreat to your door, leaving it ajar a crack, ear straining. You hear his door open. A long pause. His door closes softly. He has taken it.

Silence.

Not the old silence of your loneliness, but a thick, expectant silence. The hum is gone entirely. The apartment next door is a vacuum. For three days, there is nothing. No chair, no radio, no solitaire. No creak by the door. You panic. Was it too much? Did the key imply a demand? Have you shattered the delicate, wordless world you built?

On the fourth day, the silence is broken. Not by a hum, but by a single, firm knock on your own front door.

Your breath catches. This is the threshold. The game has left the elevator. The phantom is at your gate.

You walk to the door, your own life feeling more real and terrifying in this moment than it ever has. You see the shape of him through the peephole—a tall, indistinct blur. You think of the button, the bird, the feather, the key. You have built this moment, object by object.

You unlock the door. You turn the handle. You pull it open.

And there you stand, facing yourself.

Not a mirror, not a twin. You. Your own face, worn with the same quiet years, your own eyes wide with the same shock. You are holding, in your outstretched hand, the small, black, four-hole button. You are wearing the coat it came from.

The understanding crashes in, not as a wave, but as a slow, chilling tide. There is no neighbor. There never was. The hum was the building’s plumbing, its aging bones. The sounds were echoes of your own routines, distorted through vents and walls. The offerings you left, you found. The gifts you received, you placed. A loop of lonely madness, a folie à deux with yourself. You curated your own unseen existence, until the curated self showed up at the door, holding the first clue you’d ever planted.

You—the one who opened the door—stare. You—the one holding the button—smile a small, sad, knowing smile. You step forward, over the threshold. You press the button into your own hand. Your fingers close around it.

“I’ve been listening,” you say to yourself.

“I know,” you reply. “So have I.”

And as the door closes slowly on the two of you, the hum begins again. But now, you can’t tell which side of the door it’s coming from.
